title: The Tripwire Patent
text:
A fire alarm has a direct line to the emergency response system. Its meaning is fixed and written into building code, when it goes off, everyone knows what it is claiming. A camera with edge intelligence has no such line, and neither does a building full of IoT sensors. It is not that the plumbing is hard to build. The problem is that nobody can say ahead of time what sequence of events, observed by a system of sensors, should be allowed to trigger a 911 workflow. A person detected at 2 am, a loud sound, a temperature ramp, each of these means something different in every building, on every night.
The patent I worked on at Motorola Solutions gets around this by bootstrapping from human judgement. It was granted this May as US 12,634,398 B2 (grant PDF), and the invention won Motorola’s internal Patent of the Year award in 2022.

title: The Tripwire Patent
text:
How it works
Someone calls 911, or an alarm with a defined meaning goes off. That is the anchor, a human or a codified device has declared that a real incident exists. The system then pulls the sensor data tied to that incident’s time and place, what the nearby cameras, microphones and other sensors observed. From that metadata it arms a trigger with two parts: an alarm-escalating condition describing what this incident getting worse would look like in the sensors’ terms, and the type of response workflow that escalation would call for, staged and ready.



The alarm-escalating trigger, step by step.


Then the system watches. If later sensor data meets the armed condition, the workflow starts on its own, carrying the context responders need. The sensors, which had no standing to summon a response a minute earlier, now have a narrow and specific one, earned from the incident itself.



The workflow launches at the moment of actual escalation.


The way I describe it to friends is a tripwire. The 911 call decides where the wire is laid and what should happen when it is crossed. If nothing ever crosses it, nobody is bothered.

title: The Tripwire Patent
text:
Why I like this design
The obvious approach to connecting smart sensors to emergency response is to define, sensor by sensor, what counts as an emergency, and it does not survive contact with the real world. This design skips the general question entirely. Each real incident defines, locally and temporarily, what escalation means for that place and that moment, and the sensor system is trusted only that far.
This was joint work with my co-inventors Emmy Beltre, Kaveh Malakuti and Mariya Bondareva. Filed September 2023, published April 2025, granted May 2026.

title: Anatomy of Learning, and Downsides of Being a Second-Hand Thinker
text:
Growing up, I had a constant need of understanding how objects worked, I was constantly curious, was good at math in my peer group and dug my heels in to understand the core elements of any topic.
There though when I hit a wall for long time to learn a more complex topic, I had to make concessions on asking about the why, and accepting what was told as truth, that's just how it works and proceeding to use this assumed bedrock to then build the concepts atop.
This particularly happened to keep the pace with the fire hose of syllabus coming towards me, the need to keep with the pace of learning the course for the tests and an incomplete and immature system to digest the syllabus with a pin or a bookmark.
Now as I am older and am learning purely for the sake of understanding and out of curiosity for my selfish wants. I want to learn the concepts to their depth. I am not bound by a deadline on within which I have to learn, rather am bound by my limited time — limitations bring out efficiency. I hope to make use of my limited time well by making this an efficient use of time.

title: Anatomy of Learning, and Downsides of Being a Second-Hand Thinker
text:
Downside of Second-Hand Thinking

Be a source, not a relay. — Grant Sanderson

By not understanding a topic completely and relying on another person’s explanation, I will not understand the topic at hand. I have not played with it enough to find the nook and cranny of the thought, the way the concept grows and expands, the beauty of it or where its weaknesses lie and where it would collapse. All these aspects are fun to understand, and give a different perspective to the topic at hand as well as a different view of the world.

title: About Me
text:
I am Dharanish Kedarisetti, based in Boston. Applied ML engineer with 5+ years building production models and edge systems — currently a Research Software Engineer at Amazon working on smart cart technology.
Interests: Machine Learning, Climbing, Meditation, and Photography.

title: About Me
text:
Experience
Amazon — Research Software Engineer II, Boston, MA (Oct 2022–present) Smart cart technology (Dash Carts). Rust software for 1k–5k edge devices on AWS, shopping receipt diagnosis, CI/CD pipelines, Qualcomm NNP integration, and custom camera driver development.
Motorola Solutions — Sr. Machine Learning Engineer, Somerville, MA (Jan 2020–Sept 2022) Smart camera features for context similarity, unusual activity detection, and crowd analysis. CNN model deployment via ONNX, Docker, and Kubernetes. Contributed to the patent committee.
Avigilon — Research Engineer / Software Engineer, Somerville, MA (Feb 2018–Jan 2020) Video analytics framework development focused on unusual activity detection in C++ and Python.

title: About Me
text:
Education
Northeastern University, Boston, MA — M.Sc., Telecommunication Systems Management, 2017
BITS Pilani, India — M.S., Physics (Honors) & B.E., Electronics & Instrumentation (Honors), 2013

title: About Me
text:
Patents

US11303877B2 — Method and System for Enhancing Use of Two-Dimensional Video Analytics by Using Depth Data (Avigilon Corp, granted 2022). Improves 2D video analytics by fusing depth data to validate object classifications, correcting for shadows, reflections, and distance-based size distortions. With Pietro Russo, Peter L. Venetianer, Mahesh Saptharishi.
US20210350117A1 — Anomalous Pose Detection Method and System (Motorola Solutions, 2021). Uses neural networks to generate skeletal pose vectors and flags deviations from learned normal-behavior clusters in real time. With Teresa Dankel, Patrick Henson, Aral Sarrafi.
WO2020210504A1 / US11302117B2 — Anomaly Detection Method, System and Computer Readable Medium (Avigilon Corp / Motorola Solutions, granted 2022). Detects unusual behavior in surveillance video by modeling statistical baselines from object metadata (class, location, velocity) per scene cell — without processing raw frames. With Peter Venetianer.
WO2020051704A1 — System and Method for Improving Speed of Similarity Based Searches (Avigilon Corp, 2020). Accelerates appearance-search queries by k-medoid clustering of selected feature vectors before database search, reducing compute without sacrificing result quality. With Nicholas John Alcock, Peter L. Venetianer.
US12634398B2 (grant PDF) — Method of Generating a Trigger Initiating an Emergency Incident Response Workflow (Motorola Solutions, granted 2026). Bootstraps smart sensors into emergency response: a 911-reported incident anchors an alarm-escalating trigger armed from sensor metadata at that time and location, and the matched response workflow launches automatically when later sensor data meets the armed condition. With Emmy Beltre, Kaveh Malakuti, Mariya Bondareva. Motorola Solutions Patent of the Year award, 2022. Explained on the blog.

title: About Me
text:
Papers

Large-scale Indoor Mapping with Failure Detection and Recovery in SLAM — Sharmin Rahman, Rob DiPietro, Dharanish Kedarisetti, Vinod Kulathumani. IROS 2024. Proposes an automated visual-inertial SLAM service with a health tracker for faulty measurements and continuous session merging, validated in grocery stores spanning 1700–3700 m². [PDF] [Amazon Science]
Plus Disease in Retinopathy of Prematurity: Improving Diagnosis by Ranking Disease Severity and Using Quantitative Image Analysis — Jayashree Kalpathy-Cramer, J Peter Campbell, Deniz Erdogmus, Peng Tian, Dharanish Kedarisetti, et al. Ophthalmology 123(11):2345–2351, 2016. Shows that while experts disagree on categorical ROP diagnosis, pairwise severity ranking yields good inter-expert correlation (mean r = 0.84), and that computer-based image analysis can model this continuous spectrum. [DOI] [PubMed]
